explicit_text: My name is Minoru Francis Hayashi. I was born on August 3, 1897, in Tafuse, Yamaguchi Prefecture, Japan. When I was naturalized in the United States, I added the middle name Francis. I grew up partly in Japan and partly in the U.S., raised by my grandparents after my parents moved to America. I attended junior high school here and have many memories of both countries.

I attended junior high school here and have many memories of both countries.

explicit_text: I come from Yamaguchi Prefecture, specifically the town called Tafuse. It’s notable because former Prime Ministers Nobusuke Kishi and Eisaku Satō are from the same town. My family roots and many connections, including my wife’s family, are tied to this prefecture. My elder brother worked as a teacher and later a prefectural supervisor there.

explicit_text: Tafuse is the town in Yamaguchi Prefecture where I was born and raised. It’s a small place but significant because it produced notable figures like Kishi and Satō. My family home was built on the property of an old temple called Rin-ji in Tafuse. I have fond memories of my childhood there, including climbing trees and eating local peaches.

explicit_text: Nobusuke Kishi was from my hometown Tafuse in Yamaguchi Prefecture. He was two years older than me and was adopted by his uncle, which changed his family name to Kishi. I went to junior high school with his younger brother, Eisaku Satō, and we respected Kishi for being bright. He was a notable figure even in our school days.

explicit_text: My youngest brother graduated from the University of California before going back to Japan. He worked as an assistant to an English professor in Sendai but sadly passed away in February 191_. The university was an important step for him and connected our family to educational opportunities in the United States.

explicit_text: My grandfather was originally a priest of the Shingon sect before the Meiji period religious reforms closed his temple, Rin-ji. After the temple was closed, he became a layman but still practiced Shingon Buddhism rituals like sutra recitation and morning worship. The sect influenced our family’s values, especially cleanliness and discipline.
implicit_text: My grandfather was originally a priest of a major esoteric Buddhist sect before the religious reforms of the late 19th century closed his temple, Rin-ji. After the temple was closed, he became a layman but still practiced the sect’s rituals like sutra recitation and morning worship. The sect influenced our family’s values, especially cleanliness and discipline.

explicit_text: During the Meiji period, there was a religious reformation that affected my grandfather’s temple, Rin-ji, which was closed down. This forced him to become a layman and take on the family name Hayashi. The changes during this period had a lasting impact on our family’s history and identity.

explicit_text: I remember celebrating Japan’s victory in the Russo-Japanese War in 1905 at my elementary school. During the war, we kept images of Buddha and Jizo at home because our house was formerly a temple. Even as a small child, I prayed for Japan’s victory when passing the Jizo statue, showing how conscious I was of the war.

This practice reflected the religious traditions passed down from my grandfather and the temple heritage of our home.

explicit_text: My parents wrote to me about the San Francisco earthquake of 1906. They described how they evacuated carrying suitcases tied with ropes, which contained emergency food. I also saw slides at school in Japan showing the disaster, which made a strong impression on me as a child.
